checks.name: canonical authority and tenant isolation
checks.summary: Trusted code constructs mandatory scope filters and PostgreSQL re-resolves every candidate against current canonical identity, lifecycle, source, and authority.

checks.name: derived-store distrust
checks.summary: Malformed, duplicate, ambiguous, stale, cross-scope, hash-mismatched, missing, and tombstoned Qdrant payloads cannot become accepted context.

checks.name: provider and credential boundary
checks.summary: The Go adapter uses the standard AWS SDK credential chain, rejects legacy Coolify Bedrock bearer variables, accepts no secret flags, validates model output shape, and excludes credentials and vectors from receipts.

checks.name: private evaluation isolation
checks.summary: Public Git contains only the protocol and synthetic fixtures. Private plans, labels, queries, captures, and provider outputs are denied by repository tracking checks.

checks.name: content-free observability
checks.summary: Retrieval metrics and traces use bounded labels and counts while excluding query text, source bodies, vectors, paths, entity names, payloads, and credentials.

residual_risks: No private holdout or OOD quality result exists yet; production retrieval activation remains prohibited until Sprint 10.
A deployment workload identity and its least-privilege Bedrock policy have not been proven on the Hostinger workload.
The AMD competition profile requires local Radeon language-model and embedding inference and must keep the Bedrock adapter disabled for core workflows.
Qdrant and Neo4j administrators remain trusted infrastructure roles outside application controls.
The installed Qdrant validation wrapper depends on caller working directory and must be repaired before it can become Sprint 10 evidence.
